title: Working with a farrier: what to expect
subtitle: Hoof care for horses and donkeys on a smallholding, and how to prepare for a farrier visit.
excerpt: Horses and donkeys kept on a smallholding need their hooves trimmed or reshod every six to eight weeks. Understanding what a farrier does, how to prepare your animal, and what signs indicate a problem between visits will help you get the most from each appointment and catch developing issues early.
sourceNotes: Worshipful Company of Farriers, guidance for horse owners. British Horse Society, hoof care factsheets. Donkey Sanctuary, hoof and foot care guides.

glossaryTerms.definition: A trained professional who trims, balances, and shoes horses' hooves. In the UK, farriers must be registered with the Farriers Registration Council under the Farriers (Registration) Act 1975. Unregistered shoeing of horses is illegal.
The correct alignment of the hoof to the horse's limb, in both lateral and medial-lateral planes. An unbalanced hoof places uneven stress on joints and tendons and is a common cause of lameness.

A fungal and bacterial infection that enters the hoof through a separation or breach in the white line (the junction between the hoof wall and the sole). Visible as crumbling, discoloured material at the base of the hoof wall. Treated by cutting away the affected wall and cleaning the cavity.

content.text: must by law be registered with the Farriers Registration Council. When booking a new farrier, ask for their FRC registration number. Horses and donkeys on soft, wet grazing typically need trimming every six to eight weeks; horses in regular road work may need shoeing more frequently.

Have the animal caught, haltered, and standing in a firm, level, well-lit area before the farrier arrives. Loose boxes or a yard with a non-slip surface are ideal; a muddy field is not. An animal that cannot be caught or held reliably wastes the farrier's time and is a safety risk. If your horse or donkey is not accustomed to having its feet picked up and held, practise this daily in the weeks before the appointment.
The farrier removes the old shoe if present, cuts back the hoof wall with nippers, rasps the hoof to restore
, and either leaves the hoof unshod (barefoot trim) or fits a new shoe. A good farrier will check all four feet for signs of thrush, bruising, or
and point these out. Ask questions; this is a good opportunity to learn.

Pick out hooves daily to remove mud, stones, and dung from the frog and cleft. Look for foul smell (thrush), soft or crumbling areas around the white line, heat in the hoof wall, or any discharge. Thrush in wet conditions is common on small holdings where horses stand in mud; it responds well to cleaning and a proprietary thrush treatment but will recur unless the ground is managed. A shoe that twists, loses a nail, or is cast (lost entirely) should be seen by the farrier within a few days rather than left.

Donkey hooves grow differently from horse hooves: they are harder and more upright. Donkeys are prone to laminitis if kept on lush improved pasture, and their hooves should never be cut to the flat profile of a horse. A farrier with donkey experience is worth seeking; the Donkey Sanctuary maintains a referral list. Donkeys living on very dry ground may go longer between trims than horses, but hooves should still be inspected every six weeks.
